name: Stolen Authentication Credentials
description: External attacker with network access can steal authentication credentials by intercepting unencrypted communication channels, leading to unauthorized access to user accounts and data.
mitigations: Implement TLS/SSL for all communications
Use certificate pinning for mobile applications
Implement multi-factor authentication
Monitor for suspicious login patterns

name: Session Token Hijacking
description: Malicious user with access to network traffic can hijack active session tokens through man-in-the-middle attacks, leading to session takeover and unauthorized actions.
mitigations: Use secure, HTTP-only cookies
Implement short session timeouts
Regenerate session IDs after authentication
Use anti-CSRF tokens for state-changing operations

name: API Key Impersonation
description: Internal user with access to configuration files can extract and use API keys to impersonate legitimate services, bypassing authentication controls.
mitigations: Store API keys in secure secret management systems
Implement API key rotation policies
Use short-lived tokens instead of long-lived keys
Monitor API usage patterns for anomalies

name: DNS Spoofing and Cache Poisoning
description: Attacker with network access can poison DNS caches or forge DNS responses to redirect users to malicious servers, intercepting credentials and sensitive data.
mitigations: Implement DNSSEC validation
Use DNS-over-HTTPS or DNS-over-TLS
Configure short DNS TTL values for critical services
Monitor DNS resolution patterns for anomalies

name: OAuth Token Theft via Redirect Manipulation
description: Attacker can manipulate OAuth redirect URIs to intercept authorization codes or access tokens, gaining unauthorized access to user accounts on the target application.
mitigations: Implement strict redirect URI validation with exact matching
Use PKCE for all OAuth flows
Validate state parameter to prevent CSRF
Register and whitelist all allowed redirect URIs

name: Service-to-Service Identity Spoofing
description: Compromised microservice can impersonate other internal services due to lack of mutual authentication, accessing sensitive data and performing unauthorized operations.
mitigations: Implement mutual TLS (mTLS) between services
Use service mesh with identity-aware routing
Deploy service-level RBAC with unique identities
Monitor inter-service communication patterns

name: SQL Injection Attack
description: External attacker with application access can inject malicious SQL through input fields, leading to unauthorized data modification or deletion in the database.
mitigations: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements
Implement input validation and sanitization
Apply principle of least privilege for database accounts
Conduct regular security code reviews

name: Message Queue Poisoning
description: Compromised service with queue access can inject malicious messages into message queues, causing downstream services to process corrupted data or commands.
mitigations: Implement message signing and verification
Use authentication for queue access
Validate all message content before processing
Implement message schema validation

name: Configuration File Tampering
description: Insider with file system access can modify configuration files to alter application behavior, disable security controls, or inject malicious settings.
mitigations: Implement file integrity monitoring
Use read-only file systems where possible
Digitally sign configuration files
Apply strict access controls to configuration directories

name: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
description: Attacker hosting a malicious website can trick authenticated users into submitting unintended requests to the target application, performing state-changing operations without user consent.
mitigations: Implement anti-CSRF tokens on all state-changing forms
Use SameSite cookie attribute
Verify Origin and Referer headers
Require re-authentication for sensitive operations

name: CI/CD Pipeline Tampering
description: Attacker with access to CI/CD configuration can modify build scripts, inject malicious steps, or alter deployment artifacts, compromising the entire software supply chain.
mitigations: Require code review for pipeline configuration changes
Implement pipeline-as-code with version control
Use signed commits and artifact verification
Enforce branch protection rules on main branches

name: Insecure Deserialization
description: Attacker can supply crafted serialized objects that execute arbitrary code during deserialization, leading to remote code execution or application logic bypass.
mitigations: Avoid deserializing untrusted data
Use safe serialization formats (JSON) over binary formats
Implement integrity checks on serialized data
Apply type constraints during deserialization

name: Missing Audit Logs
description: Malicious user performing unauthorized actions can exploit missing or incomplete audit logging to deny their actions, preventing incident response and forensics.
mitigations: Implement comprehensive audit logging for all sensitive operations
Use tamper-proof logging infrastructure
Include timestamps, user IDs, and action details in logs
Regularly review and analyze audit logs

name: Log Injection
description: Attacker with application access can inject false entries into application logs by manipulating input fields, creating false audit trails and hiding malicious activity.
mitigations: Sanitize all user input before logging
Use structured logging formats (JSON)
Implement log aggregation with centralized storage
Use digital signatures for log entries

name: Transaction Repudiation
description: User initiating financial transactions can later deny their actions due to weak non-repudiation controls, leading to disputes and potential fraud.
mitigations: Implement digital signatures for transactions
Require multi-factor authentication for sensitive operations
Maintain detailed transaction logs with user verification
Use blockchain or immutable ledgers for critical transactions

name: Administrative Action Repudiation
description: Administrator performing privileged operations can deny their actions when shared admin accounts or insufficient logging make it impossible to attribute changes to individuals.
mitigations: Eliminate shared admin accounts with individual credentials
Log all admin actions with user identity and IP address
Require approval workflows for destructive operations
Implement session recording for privileged access

name: Unsigned API Request Repudiation
description: API consumer making malicious or erroneous requests can deny responsibility when requests lack cryptographic signatures or verifiable client identity.
mitigations: Implement request signing with HMAC or asymmetric keys
Record complete request/response pairs for sensitive endpoints
Use mutual TLS for client identity verification
Include correlation IDs for end-to-end request tracing

name: Database Credential Exposure
description: External attacker with code repository access can extract database credentials from configuration files or environment variables, leading to unauthorized data access.
mitigations: Never store credentials in source code or version control
Use environment variables or secret management systems
Rotate credentials regularly
Implement database encryption at rest

name: API Response Information Leakage
description: Unauthenticated user probing API endpoints can extract sensitive information from verbose error messages or detailed API responses, revealing system architecture.
mitigations: Implement generic error messages for external users
Sanitize API responses to remove sensitive data
Use API response filtering based on user permissions
Conduct regular API security testing

name: Backup Data Exposure
description: Attacker with cloud storage access can download unencrypted backup files containing sensitive customer data, leading to large-scale data breach.
mitigations: Encrypt all backups with strong encryption
Implement strict access controls on backup storage
Use separate encryption keys for backups
Regularly test backup restoration procedures

name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: Attacker can manipulate server-side URL parameters to force the application to make requests to internal services or cloud metadata endpoints, exposing sensitive infrastructure data.
mitigations: Validate and whitelist allowed outbound URLs
Block requests to internal IP ranges and metadata endpoints
Use a dedicated egress proxy for external requests
Implement network segmentation to limit internal access

name: Local File Inclusion via Directory Traversal
description: Attacker can inject ../ sequences into filesystem operations to read arbitrary files such as /etc/passwd, .env, or database credentials, exfiltrating sensitive data from the server.
mitigations: Validate and canonicalize all file paths
Use chroot or sandbox environments for file access
Implement strict allowlists for accessible directories
Remove directory listing from web servers

name: Side-Channel Information Leakage
description: Attacker can extract sensitive data by analyzing timing differences, error messages, or resource consumption patterns in application responses, bypassing direct access controls.
mitigations: Implement constant-time comparison for secrets
Normalize response times for sensitive operations
Use generic error responses that do not vary by input
Monitor for timing-based enumeration patterns

name: Resource Exhaustion Attack
description: External attacker flooding API endpoints can exhaust system resources through excessive requests, causing service degradation or complete outage for legitimate users.
mitigations: Implement rate limiting per user/IP
Use API throttling and quotas
Deploy DDoS protection services
Implement auto-scaling for high-traffic scenarios

name: Database Connection Pool Exhaustion
description: Malicious actor sending high-volume database queries can exhaust connection pools, preventing legitimate transactions from accessing the database.
mitigations: Implement connection pool limits and timeouts
Use connection pooling with proper configuration
Monitor database connection usage
Implement query timeout limits

name: Slowloris Attack
description: Attacker sending slow, incomplete HTTP requests can keep server connections open, eventually exhausting server capacity and denying service to legitimate users.
mitigations: Configure short request timeouts
Limit concurrent connections per IP
Use reverse proxy with connection limits
Deploy load balancers with request filtering

name: Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS)
description: Attacker supplying crafted input strings can trigger catastrophic backtracking in poorly written regular expressions, causing CPU exhaustion and application hang.
mitigations: Audit regular expressions for catastrophic backtracking
Use non-backtracking regex engines where available
Implement input length limits before regex matching
Set execution timeouts for regex operations

name: XML External Entity (XXE) Bomb
description: Attacker submitting crafted XML with recursive entity definitions can cause exponential memory consumption during parsing, crashing the application server.
mitigations: Disable external entity processing in XML parsers
Use JSON instead of XML where possible
Implement input size limits for XML payloads
Use safe XML parsing libraries with default-secure settings

name: Memory Exhaustion via Large Payloads
description: Attacker submitting excessively large file uploads or request bodies can exhaust server memory, causing out-of-memory crashes and service unavailability.
mitigations: Enforce strict request body size limits
Use streaming parsers for large file processing
Implement disk-based buffering for uploads
Monitor memory usage with automatic circuit breakers

name: Privilege Escalation via API
description: Low-privileged user exploiting API parameter manipulation can escalate privileges by modifying role or permission parameters in API requests.
mitigations: Implement server-side authorization checks
Never trust client-side role/permission data
Use role-based access control (RBAC)
Audit all privilege-related operations

name: Container Escape
description: Attacker with container access exploiting kernel vulnerabilities can escape container isolation, gaining host system access with elevated privileges.
mitigations: Run containers with minimal privileges
Use security profiles (AppArmor, SELinux)
Keep container runtime updated
Implement container security scanning

name: JWT Privilege Escalation
description: Attacker obtaining valid JWT token can modify claims to elevate privileges due to weak signature verification or missing validation checks.
mitigations: Use strong signing algorithms (RS256, not HS256)
Validate all JWT claims on the server side
Implement short token expiration times
Use asymmetric keys for JWT signing

name: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
description: Authenticated user can access or modify other users' resources by manipulating object identifiers in API requests due to missing authorization checks at the object level.
mitigations: Implement object-level authorization checks
Use indirect references or UUIDs instead of sequential IDs
Validate resource ownership on every request
Conduct automated IDOR testing in CI pipeline

name: Forced Browsing to Admin Endpoints
description: Attacker can guess or enumerate hidden administrative URLs and API routes that lack proper authorization checks, gaining access to privileged functionality such as user management or configuration panels.
mitigations: Enforce authentication and authorization on all admin routes
Use non-guessable URL prefixes for administrative interfaces
Implement framework-level route guards for privileged endpoints
Block unauthorized access attempts at the web application firewall

name: Dependency Confusion Attack
description: Attacker publishing malicious packages with internal package names to public registries can cause build systems to pull the malicious version, executing arbitrary code with build system privileges.
mitigations: Pin all dependencies to exact versions with integrity hashes
Configure private registry priority over public registries
Use namespace scoping for internal packages
Audit dependency resolution during builds

name: Email Spoofing and Phishing
description: Attacker can forge email headers to impersonate trusted senders, delivering phishing payloads that trick users into revealing credentials, clicking malicious links, or executing malware.
mitigations: Implement SPF, DKIM, and DMARC email authentication
Deploy email content filtering and link scanning
Conduct phishing awareness training for users
Use email gateway with sender reputation scoring

name: IP Address Spoofing
description: Attacker can forge source IP addresses in network packets to bypass IP-based access controls, evade logging attribution, or amplify reflected denial-of-service attacks.
mitigations: Implement ingress filtering (BCP38) at network boundary
Use application-layer authentication instead of IP-based trust
Deploy reverse path forwarding verification
Avoid relying solely on IP addresses for access control

name: TLS Certificate Forgery
description: Attacker with access to a compromised or rogue certificate authority can issue fraudulent TLS certificates, enabling transparent interception of encrypted communications.
mitigations: Implement Certificate Transparency log monitoring
Use certificate pinning for critical connections
Deploy CAA DNS records to restrict allowed certificate authorities
Monitor for unauthorized certificates via CT log alerts

name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
description: Attacker can inject malicious JavaScript into web pages viewed by other users through unsanitized inputs, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, and redirection to malicious sites.
mitigations: Implement context-aware output encoding for all user-controlled data
Use Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict script execution
Sanitize rich text input with allowlist-based HTML sanitizers
Enable HTTP-only and Secure flags on session cookies

name: Malicious File Upload
description: Attacker can upload files containing executable code, web shells, or malware by bypassing insufficient file type validation, leading to remote code execution on the server.
mitigations: Validate file types using content inspection not just extensions
Store uploaded files outside the web root in isolated storage
Scan all uploads with antivirus and sandboxing
Enforce strict file size limits and rename uploaded files

name: HTTP Request Smuggling
description: Attacker can exploit discrepancies in how front-end proxies and back-end servers parse HTTP request boundaries, smuggling requests that bypass security controls and access unauthorized resources.
mitigations: Normalize HTTP parsing between proxy and backend servers
Disable HTTP/1.1 connection reuse where feasible
Use HTTP/2 end-to-end to eliminate ambiguous framing
Deploy WAF rules that detect request smuggling patterns

name: Prototype Pollution
description: Attacker can manipulate JavaScript object prototypes through unsanitized input merging, causing application-wide behavior changes including authentication bypass and remote code execution.
mitigations: Use Object.create(null) for lookup maps instead of plain objects
Validate and sanitize all keys in recursive merge operations
Freeze Object.prototype in application initialization
Use Map instead of plain objects for user-controlled key-value data

name: Timestamp Manipulation for Repudiation
description: Attacker with system access can manipulate local clock or NTP responses to falsify event timestamps, undermining audit trail integrity and forensic timeline reconstruction.
mitigations: Use authenticated NTP sources with NTS protocol
Implement server-side timestamping for all audit events
Deploy trusted timestamping services for critical records
Cross-reference timestamps across multiple independent sources

name: Multi-Party Transaction Denial
description: In multi-party workflows, any participant can deny their involvement or claim different terms were agreed upon when the system lacks cryptographic commitment from each party.
mitigations: Require digital signatures from each party at every step
Implement commitment schemes with verifiable receipts
Use append-only audit logs with independent witnesses
Record cryptographic hashes of agreed terms at each stage

name: TLS Downgrade Attack
description: Attacker with network position can force clients and servers to negotiate weaker TLS versions or cipher suites, enabling decryption of sensitive communications.
mitigations: Disable TLS 1.0 and 1.1, enforce TLS 1.2+ minimum
Implement TLS_FALLBACK_SCSV to prevent downgrade attacks
Configure strong cipher suite ordering with forward secrecy
Use HSTS headers to prevent HTTP fallback

name: Memory Dump Leakage
description: Attacker with access to crash dumps, core files, or swap space can extract secrets, session tokens, and encryption keys from unprotected process memory.
mitigations: Disable core dumps in production environments
Use memory-safe secret storage that zeros on deallocation
Encrypt swap partitions and hibernation files
Restrict access to debug and diagnostic endpoints

name: Cloud Metadata Service Exposure
description: Application running in cloud environments can inadvertently expose the instance metadata service, allowing attackers to retrieve IAM credentials, instance identity tokens, and infrastructure configuration.
mitigations: Use IMDSv2 requiring session tokens for metadata access
Block metadata endpoint access from application containers
Implement network policies preventing 169.254.169.254 access
Use IAM roles with minimum required permissions

name: Hash Collision Denial of Service
description: Attacker can craft inputs that produce hash collisions in hash table implementations, degrading O(1) lookups to O(n) and causing severe CPU exhaustion in request processing.
mitigations: Use randomized hash functions with per-process seeds
Limit the number of parameters in HTTP requests
Implement request processing timeouts
Use hash table implementations with collision-resistant algorithms

name: Disk Space Exhaustion
description: Attacker can trigger excessive log generation, fill upload storage, or abuse caching mechanisms to exhaust available disk space, causing application crashes and data loss.
mitigations: Implement log rotation with size-based limits
Set per-user and per-directory storage quotas
Monitor disk usage with alerts at threshold levels
Use separate partitions for application logs and data storage

name: WebSocket Flooding
description: Attacker can open numerous WebSocket connections or send high-volume messages over persistent connections, exhausting server memory and connection handling capacity.
mitigations: Implement per-client connection limits and message rate throttling
Set maximum message size and connection idle timeouts
Use authentication before allowing WebSocket upgrade
Monitor and alert on abnormal WebSocket connection patterns

name: Kernel Exploit for Privilege Escalation
description: Attacker with local code execution can exploit unpatched kernel vulnerabilities to escalate from unprivileged user to root, gaining full control over the host system.
mitigations: Apply kernel security patches promptly
Use kernel hardening features (KASLR, SMEP, SMAP)
Deploy mandatory access control (SELinux, AppArmor) to limit exploit impact
Minimize kernel attack surface by disabling unused modules

name: Race Condition Privilege Escalation
description: Attacker can exploit time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) race conditions in authorization checks, performing privileged operations by racing between the permission check and the action.
mitigations: Use atomic operations for check-then-act authorization patterns
Implement file locking for filesystem-based access control
Use database transactions with serializable isolation for critical operations
Design authorization checks to be idempotent and re-verified at execution

name: Misconfigured IAM Roles and Policies
description: Overly permissive IAM roles or policies can allow users or services to assume higher privileges than intended, accessing restricted resources or performing administrative actions.
mitigations: Apply principle of least privilege to all IAM policies
Conduct regular IAM access reviews and remove unused permissions
Use IAM Access Analyzer to detect overly broad policies
Implement permission boundaries to cap maximum privilege levels
